You know that moment right before you step out onto a stage for the first time? Your stomach drops and well, every rational part of your brain is screaming at you to just turn around and run. Oh, yeah, it is a deeply primal response. You are basically standing right on the edge of the void. Right, exactly. And that exact moment of panic is, well, that is what we are exploring today in this deep dive. We are looking at two classic stories of flight. Liam O'Flaherty's, his first flight, and Frederick Forsythe's, the Black Aeroplane. Yes. And our mission today is to figure out, is fear an actual barrier or is it a necessary biological step toward growth? It is a fascinating question. And we start with O'Flaherty Siegel, who is, you know, entirely paralyzed on a cliff ledge. He is absolutely terrified of that massive expanse of the sea below him. Exactly. And his family's initial response to this paralysis is, well, it is essentially extreme tough love. Yeah, they scold him and literally threaten him with starvation. Right. But yelling at someone to not be afraid doesn't just magically cure their fear. No, definitely not. So the mother shifts tactics. She uses sheer desperation by dangling a piece of fish just out of reach of the starving bird. I mean, I love this part because it makes me think of fear like a software program. Oh, how so? Well, the fear is the software telling the system, you know, do not jump, it is dangerous. But extreme hunger acts like a hardware override. That is a great way to put it. Yeah, it just bypasses the brain entirely to force those wings to open. And that is exactly how the biology works here. I mean, she doesn't hand him the solution. She creates a void. Right. Hunger. Exactly. The extreme necessity of hunger pushes the bird past his intellect. So that minute of monstrous terror he feels during the freefall isn't the sign he is failing. It is a requirement. Yes, it is the biological prerequisite. It is the threshold he absolutely must physically cross to unlock the instinct to fly. OK, so biology and instinct can override fear when we are pushed off a literal ledge. Right. But what happens to us humans when biology just isn't enough? When we have already taken the leap. Exactly. Like when our technology and our rationality completely fail us out there in that void. Well, that brings us perfectly to our second source, the black airplane. A total shift in tone. Yeah, we shift from biological instinct to the strict limits of rational control. We have a pilot flying an old Dakota plane from France to England. And he is highly motivated by something entirely mundane, right? Oh, yeah, he just really wants to get home for a full English breakfast. I mean, we have all been there. Definitely. But he sees these massive mountain like storm clouds ahead. And instead of turning back, he just risks it and flies straight in. Which is when his reality basically breaks down. Total darkness. His compass spins wildly and just dies and his radio goes completely dead. Total isolation. Exactly. But then out of nowhere, another plane appears beside him. The black airplane. Right. The pilot of this mysterious black plane silently gestures for him to follow. But and here is the kicker. The text explicitly notes a truly bizarre detail. Oh, the lights. Yeah, this black plane have absolutely no lights on its wings. Wait, help me out here. It is a pitch black storm and the plane is unlit. That is what the text says. But that makes zero physical sense. I mean, is the pilot just hallucinating this out of sheer panic? This is where the story shifts from a physical crisis to a deeply psychological one. OK, how so? When the human brain is subjected to extreme stress and sensory deprivation, like, you know, being trapped in a pitch black spinning cockpit, it desperately seeks patterns. It looks for a solution that isn't there. Exactly. The brain can literally hallucinate a way out. It projects the pilot's own internal will to survive into a physical manifestation. Oh, wow. So the stranger saving him might actually just be his own subconscious throwing him a lifeline. Precisely. And the author Forsyth never confirms it either way. Right, because the excerpt ends so abruptly. It does. Right as the pilot follows this plane like an obedient child, it just stops. There is no tidy resolution at all. Which has to be deliberate, right? Oh, absolutely. The ambiguity is the entire point. It forces you, the reader, to sit with the sheer uncertainty of the unknown, just like the pilot did. That makes so much sense. You know, both of these stories forcibly strip away the illusion of safety. They really do. For the seagull, it was the solid rock of the ledge. And for the pilot, it was, well, a working compass and a radio. And when those physical, rational comforts are gone, you're left completely alone with the void. Which really makes you wonder about how we live today. I mean, we are so obsessed with safety, with GPS guarantees and having backup plans for our backup plans. We never want to be without a safety net. Exactly. But if we never allow ourselves to be stripped of that rational support, are we actually stunting our own ability to trigger those essential survival instincts? That is a really profound question. Yeah. What does it actually mean to trust something, whether it is raw instinct, a stranger or the unknown itself, when all rational support completely fails? Definitely something for you to think about the next time you find yourself standing on the edge of the unknown.
